coredata.dc:title: A specific discriminating GSH from Cys/Hcy fluorescence nanosensor: The carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Dong
Meng, Ya-ting
Zhang, Yuan
Wang, Qi
Lu, Wen-jing
Shuang, Shao-min
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel nanosensor was designed to discriminate glutathione (GSH) from cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy) with a two-stage fluorescent switch. The nanosensor (CDs-MnO2 NFs) was fabricated via an amidation coupling reaction between Carbon dots (CDs) and manganese dioxide nanoflowers (MnO2 NFs) that gave rise to the opposite fluorescent responses to GSH and Cys/Hcy. A “turn-off” state generated because the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and MnO2. The nanosensor further triggered different fluorescence behaviors in the presence of three thiols. On the one hand, GSH reduced MnO2 into Mn2+ and subsequently restored the fluorescence of CDs, concomitant “turn-off-on” fluorescence shift. On the other hand, Cys/Hcy similarly led to the decomposition of MnO2 but provoked a further fluorescence quench into the “turn-off-off” state. It turned out that the reduction product of Cys/Hcy was SO3 2- instead of disulfide bond from GSH, which further quenched the fluorescence of CDs. Thus, the discriminating GSH from Cys/Hcy was achieved based on the different oxidation degrees through the nanosensor. As a result, GSH could be sensitively detected in a proportional range of 2.0–200 μM with a LOD of 0.558 μM. Meanwhile, the sensor was applied to recognize cancer cells by monitoring GSH levels. Therefore, the nanosensor with brand new sensing principle for specific recognition of GSH, which is a good candidate for GSH sensing in biological and clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanosensors
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Manganese dioxide nanoflowers
Carbon dots
GSH sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs-MnO2 NFs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and 91843301 ), the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 201901D211454 ) and the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi (No. 2020L0006 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM picture of CDs. (B) A presents size distributions of CDs. (C) The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (D) The excitation-independent fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at the different excitation wavelengths from 340 nm to 420 nm.

(A) SEM image and (B) TEM image of MnO2 NFs. (C) TEM mapping analysis of MnO2 NFs in the dark; (D-E) Element mapping of O, Mn. (F) TEM images of CDs-MnO2 NFs. (G) An enlarged photograph of the areas in (F).
The XPS (A) full scan, (B) Mn2p, (C) C1 s and (D) O1 s spectra of CDs-MnO2 NFs.

(A) Spectral overlap showing the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs (red) and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 (black). (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs/MnO2 (black), CDs/MnO2 +GSH (red), CDs/MnO2 +Cys (blue), CDs/MnO2 +Hcy (purple). (C) The digital pictures of different assays under visible light (top) and UV light (bottom) (a: CDs; b: CDs-MnO2; c: CDs-MnO2 +GSH; d: CDs-MnO2 +Cys; e: CDs-MnO2 +Hcy). (D) The fluorescence spectra of CDs (blue), CDs+GSH (green), CDs+Cys (LT yellow), CDs+Hcy (LT cyan).
The process of MnO2 NFs reacting with GSH (A) and Cys/Hcy (B).
The XPS spectra of products Cys (A), Hcy (B) and GSH (C) react with MnO2 NFs. (D) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs+GSH, CDs+Cys, CDs+Hcy, CDs+GSSG, CDs+SO3 2-.
(A) Fluorescence stability of CDs-MnO2 NFs. (B) Optimization of the reaction time and pH (GSH: 0.1 mM, CDs-MnO2 NFs: 20 μg).

(A) Fluorescence recovery of CDs-MnO2 NFs with different concentrations of GSH (CDs-MnO2 NFs: 20 μg, λex= 380 nm; λem = 465 nm). (B) The linear calibration plot for GSH detection. (C and D) Fluorescence responses [(F-F0)/F0] of the CDs-MnO2 NFs (20 μg) toward GSH (10 μM) and interferents. (F and F0 are fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of test object, respectively).

The CLSM images of (A) PC12, (B) Hela, (C) NEM-treated PC12 and (D) NEM-treated Hela cells incubated with CDs-MnO2 NFs. From left to right, each column is blue channel, bright field and merge, respectively. The scale bars are 50 µm.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of CDs-MnO2 NFs.
Principle of the CDs-MnO2 NFs for discriminating GSH from Cys and Hcy.

head.ce:title: A specific discriminating GSH from Cys/Hcy fluorescence nanosensor: The carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan 030008, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, 030008, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel nanosensor was designed to discriminate glutathione (GSH) from cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy) with a two-stage fluorescent switch. The nanosensor (CDs-MnO2 NFs) was fabricated via an amidation coupling reaction between Carbon dots (CDs) and manganese dioxide nanoflowers (MnO2 NFs) that gave rise to the opposite fluorescent responses to GSH and Cys/Hcy. A “turn-off” state generated because the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and MnO2. The nanosensor further triggered different fluorescence behaviors in the presence of three thiols. On the one hand, GSH reduced MnO2 into Mn2+ and subsequently restored the fluorescence of CDs, concomitant “turn-off-on” fluorescence shift. On the other hand, Cys/Hcy similarly led to the decomposition of MnO2 but provoked a further fluorescence quench into the “turn-off-off” state. It turned out that the reduction product of Cys/Hcy was SO3 2- instead of disulfide bond from GSH, which further quenched the fluorescence of CDs. Thus, the discriminating GSH from Cys/Hcy was achieved based on the different oxidation degrees through the nanosensor. As a result, GSH could be sensitively detected in a proportional range of 2.0–200 μM with a LOD of 0.558 μM. Meanwhile, the sensor was applied to recognize cancer cells by monitoring GSH levels. Therefore, the nanosensor with brand new sensing principle for specific recognition of GSH, which is a good candidate for GSH sensing in biological and clinical applications.

The nanosensor discriminated GSH from Cys/Hcy with a two-stage fluorescent switch.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Biothiols, including glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy), their transport and metabolism play important roles in many physiological processes and diseases [1–3]. Among them, GSH is usually the highest level of biothiols in living cells (about 1–10 mM) [4], which is related to biological processes including homeostasis, metabolism, signal transduction and gene regulation [5–8]. Furthermore, deficiency and excess of GSH are closely associated with the development of various diseases, such as cancer, AIDS, liver damage, diabetes, and aging etc. [9–12]. Thus, the qualitative and quantitative detection of GSH has become more and more critical in the biological system.

Numerous analytical methods, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), mass spectrometry (MS), surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) etc. have been utilized for detecting GSH [13–16]. Although these conventional methods perform well in GSH detection, some of these confronted drawbacks of complex sample pretreatment, high cost and time-consuming process [17]. Comparing, fluorescence analysis is more competitive with the distinct characteristics of high sensitivity, easy operation, and cost effective [18,19]. However, discriminating GSH over Cys/Hcy is still challengeable due to their similar structure with thiol, carboxylic and amino groups [20]. In this context, a variety of organic fluorophores with the sensing mechanisms such as Michael addition [21,22], nucleophile substitution [23,24], cyclization, cleavage reaction [25,26] and so on, have come into use to specific detect GSH in succession. For example, Zou et al. [27] designed a coumarin derivative-based two-photon fluorescent probe Cou-Br. The brominated moiety of probe could be substituted by the sulfhydryl group (-SH) in GSH via a strong nucleophilic reactivity and generated the strong fluorescence. Due to the appropriate distance between the amino (-NH2) and carbonyl groups (-CO) in Cys and Hcy, 6-membered rings were formed through intramolecular condensation to provide a faint fluorescence. Therefore, the discriminative detection of GSH was realized. Liu’s group [28] synthesized a spirolactam structure NIR probe (CyR) to recognize GSH. CyR formatted unstable intermediates with thiols by the addition reaction of mercapto and aldehyde groups. For Cys and Hcy the unstable intermediates would produce nonfluorescent five-or six-ring heterocycles by rearrangement and cyclization, but the unstable intermediate from GSH occurred hydrolysis reaction and induced strong fluorescence. Yin et al. [29] reported a dual reaction site-loaded coumarin derivative probe GSH547. In the probe, the reactive chlorine acted as the nucleophilic substitution reaction site and the α, β-unsaturated ketone acted as the nucleophilic addition reaction site for the sulfhydryl and amino groups of GSH, respectively. Due to GSH has the advantage of suitable size, the probe could specifically respond to it, nor for Cys/Hcy, which achieved GSH specific detection. Unfortunately, most organic fluorophores have multistep synthetic route, long reaction time and poor water solubility [30,31], which intrinsically limited their further application.

Compared to organic fluorophores, carbon dots (CDs) possess distinct advantages due to their photo-stability, excellent biocompatibility and good chemical inertness [32–34]. More importantly, the preparation of CDs is of diverse raw materials sources [35]. These outstanding properties of CDs offer the opportunity for sensing of GSH. In 2015, Ding’s group [36]. first presented a competition assay for detecting GSH based on CDs-Hg (Ⅱ) fluorescence recovery system. Enlightened by the competition mechanism, many heavy metal ions Ag+, Cu2+, Au3+ [37–39] intermediated carbon dots as platforms for GSH detection from recovered fluorescence and extended to cell imaging. Nevertheless, heavy metal ions display toxicity and will gravely harm the environment and public health, which limit their further applications in physiological systems [40,41]. Thus, searching for an alternative quencher is essential for detecting GSH. Manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanoparticles are of broad light absorption range (250–550 nm) [42] and give rise to quench the fluorescence of CDs. These properties permit the occurrence of FRET between MnO2 nanoparticles and CDs. Based on GSH reducing MnO2 into Mn2+ [43], MnO2 mediated fluorescent “turn-on” sensor for GSH detecting. In 2015, Lin et al. [44] designed and fabricated FRET-based CDs-MnO2 nanosheets for lighting up sensing GSH. Subsequently, various shapes of MnO2 nanostructures coupled with CDs are also utilized such as MnO2 nanospheres [45], MnO2 nanorods [46], MnO2 nanoflowers [47] and so on. In these strategies, the foundation for sensing GSH is that MnO2 oxidizes GSH to glutathione disulfide (GS-SG) [48]. However, the thiols stimulated quencher degradation tend to simple turn-on fluorescence signal because all these molecules contain reductive thiols. Owing to thiols differences in spatial structure [49], MnO2 may oxidize them to different reaction products. Thus, the better understanding of the reaction of MnO2 with Cys/Hcy is significant to realize discriminating GSH from Cys/Hcy.

This work developed a novel nanosensor to discriminate GSH from Cys/Hcy through a two-stage fluorescent switch based on carbon dots-manganese dioxide nanoflowers (denoted as CDs-MnO2 NFs). GSH caused the fluorescence “turn-off-on” of the nanosensor, but Cys/Hcy made the opposite “turn-off-off” state, which achieved the discrimination of GSH. The different reduction products (GSSG from GSH and SO3 2- from Cys/Hcy) were responsible for this phenomenon. The brand new principle was proposed for the first time and CDs-MnO2 NFs with high selectivity were further applied for GSH detection and cell imaging.
The materials, instruments and methods were attached in Supporting Information (SI).
The synthetic procedures of CDs-MnO2 NFs have been described in Supplementary Information (SI).

For GSH detection, 100 µL CDs-MnO2 NFs (2 mg / mL) was added into 800 µL HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 5.5). Later, 100 µL different concentrations GSH (0–2 mM) were injected to the above solutions and incubated 5 min at room temperature. Afterward, the photoluminescence was measured with excitation at 380 nm. Other biothiols and interferences were detected following a similar process to GSH.

Initially, Hela cells and PC12 cells were cultured in culture medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a 5.0% CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 48 h. Before imaging, the cells were washed with PBS buffer three times and then incubated with CDs-MnO2 NFs (50 μg) for 5 min. Next, to eliminate intracellular GSH, the inhibition group cells were cultivated with N-ethylmaleimide (NEM, 400 μM) for 20 min, washed three times with PBS buffer, and then incubated 5 min with CDs-MnO2 NFs to image. The intracellular fluorescence images were gathered on a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs-MnO2 NFs
ce:section.ce:para: The process of fabricating CDs-MnO2 NFs was illustrated in Scheme 1. The CDs were first prepared by a green route, one-step hydrothermal reaction of L-tryptophan. Later, the MnO2 nanoflowers (MnO2 NFs) were garnered via a redox reaction in ultrapure water, in which potassium permanganate (KMnO4) as a source of manganese and oleic acid (OA) as a reductant. After that, a layer-by-layer (LBL) method was applied to introduce carboxyl groups. In this process, the negative MnO2 NFs were orderly absorbed with positive allylamine hydrochloride (PAH) and negative acrylic acid (PAA) through electrostatic interactions. Lastly, the CDs-MnO2 NFs was fabricated by an amidation coupling reaction between the surface functionalization MnO2 NFs and the CDs.

Fig. 1A indicates that the CDs mainly were spherical with fine dispersion and uniform in size, the average diameter (based on 100 particles) was 2.1 ± 0.25 nm in the range from 1.0 to 4.5 nm (Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 1C, UV-Vis spectra of CDs displayed a strong absorption peak around 350 nm. The emission spectra of CDs exhibited excitation-independent fluorescent behavior as depicted in Fig. 1D, with an emission maximum at 465 nm under 380 nm excitation.

For MnO2 nanoparticles, the SEM image ( Fig. 2A) showed that the as-fabricated MnO2 nanoparticles tended to be extremely like nanoflowers. Fig. 2B displayed a specific low-magnified TEM image of the MnO2 nanoparticles, in which MnO2 nanoparticles were equally distributed and the dimension was about 80 nm. The MnO2 nanoparticles were redouble certified by the high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HHAADF-STEM)-based elemental mapping (Fig. 2C, D, E). After bonding with CDs, the final CDs-MnO2 NFs were built up. From the TEM image (Fig. 2F and G), CDs were adhered to the surface of MnO2 NFs, and the lattice spacing of MnO2 NFs and CDs was observed to be 0.51 nm and 0.16 nm, respectively. This result indicated that the CDs were successfully attached to MnO2 NFs.

The related physicochemical properties of the nanosensor were also investigated. In Fig. S1, the step-wise altered zeta potentials indicated CDs-MnO2 NFs were successfully fabricated. The MnO2 NFs were negatively charged at − 41.5 mV and then elevated to 41.2 mV after loading PAH. Further, coating the PAA, the ζ potential was 30.1 mV. After bonding CDs, the gained CDs-MnO2 NFs held a zeta potential of 18.6 mV that proved the combination of CDs-MnO2 NFs was successful. Then, surface status information on CDs-MnO2 NFs was obtained from FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2). In Fig. S2a, it was clearly found that several main absorption peaks could be assigned as 3461 cm−1 imputed to the O-H, 3145 cm−1 belonged to the C-H, 1698 cm−1 and 1439 cm−1 were owed to the CO, and 1069 cm−1 for C-O bond, respectively. In Fig. S2b, the bands of MnO2 at 1643 cm−1 and 1457 cm−1 were generally ascribed to O-H bending vibrations. And, 516 cm−1 was attributed to the Mn-O and Mn-O-Mn vibrations. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-MnO2 NFs (Fig. S2c) stored the typical peaks of both prepared CDs and MnO2, which verified that CDs were attached to MnO2 NFs. The main elements of CDs-MnO2 NFs could be obtained in the XPS spectrum which consisted of Mn, C, O and N ( Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B showed two unique peaks at 654 eV and 642.1 eV, which owed to the Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 spin orbit peaks of MnO2. The high resolution C1 s spectrum presented a CC peak at 284.75 eV, a C-O peak at 285.6 eV and a CO peak at 288.35 eV (Fig. 3C). In Fig. 3D showed three unique peaks. The Mn-O in MnO2 was a peak of 529.9 eV, while the peaks of 531.4 eV and 533.6 eV were proved to be CO and C-O on the CDs. All the above characterization results indicated CDs hybridized MnO2.

The two-stage fluorescent switch of the nanosensor for GSH specific detection as depicted in Scheme 2. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched by MnO2 NFs to make a “turn-off” state. Furthermore, with the introduction of GSH and Cys/Hcy, the states of “turn-off-on” and “turn-off-off” were got, respectively. It was due to the reduction product of Cys/Hcy instead of GSH (MnO2 NFs oxidized GSH and Cys/Hcy to GSSG and SO3 2-, respectively), which caused two different fluorescence states with CDs.

The sensing mechanism of CDs-MnO2 NFs was investigated. From Fig. 4A, a broad UV-Vis absorption of MnO2 NFs from 300 to 500 nm, and the CDs had the highest fluorescent emission of about 465 nm under excitation at 380 nm. A significant overlap area was observe between optical absorption of MnO2 and the emission of the CDs. Moreover, the result of TEM (Fig. 2G) indicated the CDs deposited on the surface of MnO2 NFs, which could shorten and restrict the distance between CDs and MnO2 NFs to generate effective FRET [50]. Thus, we assumed that MnO2 NFs and CDs might occur FRET. Subsequently, the lifetimes of CDs and CDs-MnO2 NFs were measured (Fig. S3). As showed in Table S1, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs-MnO2 NFs were 16.06 ns and 14.49 ns, respectively. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CDs was put down to the FRET between CDs and MnO2 NFs. Next, we found that the sensor could specifically detect GSH. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-MnO2 NFs was significantly up-regulated by the addition of GSH and further reduced after the addition of Cys or Hcy (Fig. 4B). From Fig. 4C, all colors were colorless except for only CDs-MnO2 NFs, and this phenomenon proved that GSH/Cys/Hcy could degrade MnO2. And it could be seen that the fluorescence was consistent with the above fluorescence spectrum under UV light. Subsequently, the response of CDs to GSH/Cys/Hcy was investigated. When CDs added to GSH, Cys/Hcy, respectively. The fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4D) and the ultraviolet absorption (Fig. S4) of the mixture barely changed, which meant that CDs did not respond to GSH/Cys/Hcy.

Therefore, the effects of the reaction products of GSH/Cys/Hcy with MnO2 NFs were investigated. For GSH, it would be oxidized by MnO2 NFs to form GSSG, the process displayed in Fig. 5A. Firstly, the intermediate of MnⅣGS was formed rapidly (eqn (1)). Subsequently, the reaction of MnⅣGS and H2O occurred to form intermediate MnIIIOH and release GS• (eqn (2)). Then, the intermediate MnIIIOH chelated with an additional GSH molecule to form MnIIIGS, which further reacting with H2O to produce MnⅡOH and GS• (eqn (3) and (4)). Final, two GS• bonded to form the corresponding disulfide (eqn (5)) [42,51,52]. As shown in Fig. 5B, the reaction of Cys/Hcy with MnO2 NFs consisted of several stages. Initially, it was the formation of the intermediate sulfenic acid (R-SOH). Then, unstable sulfenic acid was oxidized to a more stable sulfinate (S (+4)) [22,53].

To further identify the resulting reaction products, the mixture reaction was surveyed with a high-resolution liquid chromatograph mass spectrometer (HR-LC-MS). In the HRMS analysis, GSH and MnO2 NFs reaction product GSSG was verified to have a m/z of 612.15 in the mass spectra (Fig. S5), the peak at m/z = 152.14 (Fig. S6) and m/z = 180.17 (Fig. S7) were the sulfinate that Cys/Hcy reacted with MnO2 NFs, respectively. Then the products of Cys and Hcy reacted with MnO2 NFs were obtained, and the XPS spectrum of the products presented the SO3 2- peak at 167.6 eV ( Fig. 6A) and 167.5 eV (Fig. 6B) [54]. The XPS spectrum of MnO2 NFs reacted with GSH did not present the SO3 2- peak in Fig. 6C. Thus, the results of HRMS and XPS certified that MnO2 NFs oxidized GSH and Cys/Hcy to GSSG and SO3 2-, respectively. Subsequently, the response of CDs to different products was explored. The UV absorption of GSH reacted with CDs-MnO2 was different from Cys and Hcy (Fig. S8). And in Fig. 6D, the fluorescence of CDs was significantly reduced after adding SO3 2-, but other substances have little effect on the fluorescence of CDs. The effect of some sulfur oxide anions on the fluorescence of CDs was shown in Fig. S9, it turned out that sulfur oxide anions except SO3 2- had negligible effect on the fluorescence of CDs. Thus, the CDs-MnO2 NFs could discriminate between GSH and Cys/Hcy with different fluorescence responses. It was due to the elaborately selected CDs illustrated opposite fluorescent signal (no effect for GSSG, quenched for SO3 2-).

As shown in Fig. 7A, the CDs-MnO2 NFs were stability, it indicated that the detection was not affected by the environment. Afterwards, the influences of pH and time were explored. In Fig. 7B, after 5 min, the highest fluorescence recovery efficiency of sensor could get at pH 5.5. This phenomenon illustrated that the best pH was 5.5 and the best time was 5 min to detect GSH.

To evaluate the sensor's GSH detection capability, the fluorescence recovery signals with different concentrations of GSH were measured. Fig. 8A showed the fluorescence of sensing response reached the maximum with the concentration of GSH increasing to 200 μM. Meanwhile, a good linear relationship for GSH (2.0–200 μM) with a correlation coefficient square of 0.99617 was obtained (Fig. 8B). The limit of detection was 0.558 μM (3σ). We compared the linear range and detection limit of this method with those from literatures (Table S2). It is evident that the proposed method is superior to most of the approaches for GSH detection in sensitivity or linear range. The selectivity of CDs-MnO2 NFs to detect GSH was studied against some probable coexistence contents including metal ions, amino acids. Considering the aim of CDs-MnO2 NFs is to recognize cancer cells. Ascorbic acid (AA) concentrations in cancer patients are often at deficiency concentrations [55,56], and AA concentration in plasma is at the level of 30–60 μM [57]. Thus, the interference of AA (60 μM) and other interfering molecules (1 mM) were investigated and the results were supplemented in Fig. 8C. It was found that other interferent except GSH hardly induced fluorescence enhancement. The results demonstrated that the CDs-MnO2NFs exhibited a highly selective toward GSH over other non-target samples. More importantly, Hcy and Cys produced opposite fluorescence responses (Fig. 8D). Hence, CDs-MnO2 NFs had wonderful selectivity for GSH over other non-target samples. And, it further indicated the nanosensor could discriminate GSH from Cys and Hcy.

The excellent performance of the CDs-MnO2 NFs for discrimination GSH from Cys and Hcy encouraged us to explore its imaging capability in living cells further. First, the cell viability was investigated by a standard CCK-8 assay. Hela cells and PC12 cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs-MnO2 NFs for 12 h. As shown in Fig. S10 and Fig. S11, no apparent loss of cell viability was found of exposure to the CDs-MnO2 NFs at concentrations 200 μg/mL. It’s demonstrating that the CDs-MnO2 NFs manifested low cytotoxicity. Due to the GSH level of cancer cells was higher than normal cells, we investigated the response behaviors of the sensor in living HeLa cancer cells and PC12 normal cells using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). A robust blue fluorescence emission could be visualized in Hela cells incubated with CDs-MnO2 NFs ( Fig. 9A). Conversely, the PC12 cells had only a weak insignificant fluorescence (Fig. 9B), which indicated the sensor could specifically recognize cancer cells through high expressed GSH. In order to confirm the specificity of the sensor for recognizing cancer cells, both cells were pretreated with an effective glutathione inhibitor (N-ethylmaleimide, NEM), and then incubated with CDs-MnO2 NFs for 5 min, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9C and D, an insignificant fluorescence signal was scanned for NEM-treated Hela cells, which was not evident in PC12 cells. The above results implied that the CDs-MnO2 NFs could recognize cancer cells via the intracellular GSH mediating “turn-on” fluorescence imaging.

In summary, a novel nanosensor was fabricated to discriminate GSH from Cys and Hcy based on CDs-MnO2 NFs with a two-stage fluorescent switch. The possible mechanism might be the different responses of CDs with different products. The product of GSH reacted with MnO2 NFs was GSSG, which was different from the product SO3 2- of Cys/Hcy. Importantly, the elaborately selected CDs illustrated the opposite fluorescent signal (no effect for GSSG, quenched for SO3 2-). Thus, CDs-MnO2 NFs could discriminate GSH from Cys and Hcy through the “turn-on” or “turn-off” state of CDs, respectively. Subsequently, it was successfully applied to recognize cancer cells from normal cells. As far as we know, this is the first time to propose a strategy for discriminating GSH from Cys and Hcy based on MnO2. We hope it will provide a valuable strategy for discriminating GSH and further prompt the research for exploring their relationship in the biosystem.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and 91843301), the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 201901D211454) and the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi (No. 2020L0006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132135.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of amino acids
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhou, J. Yoon, Recent progress in fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of amino acids, Chem. Soc. Rev. 2012, 41(1), 52–67.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple turn-on fluorescent sensor for discriminating Cys/Hcy and GSH from different fluorescent signals
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.B. Wang, H.J. Li, C. Liu, Y.X. Hu, M.C. Li, Y.C. Wu, Simple Turn-On Fluorescent Sensor for Discriminating Cys/Hcy and GSH from Different Fluorescent Signals, Anal. Chem. 2021, 93(4), 2244–2253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure, mechanism and regulation of peroxiredoxins
Trends Biochem. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.A. Wood, E. Schröder, J.R. Harris, L.B. Poole, Structure, mechanism and regulation of peroxiredoxins, Trends Biochem. Sci. 2003, 28(1), 32–40.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mitochondria-anchored colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent chemosensor for visualizing cysteine/homocysteine in living cells and Daphnia magna model
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yang, J. Fan, W. Sun, J. Du, Peng, X. Mitochondria-anchored colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent chemosensor for visualizing cysteine/homocysteine in living cells and Daphnia magna model, Anal. Chem. 2019, 91(19), 12531–12537.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of reversible fluorescent probes for live-cell imaging and quantification of fast glutathione dynamics
Nat. Chem.

Br. J. Nutr.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dong Wang is currently working towards a PhD of Environmental Science under the supervision of Prof. Chuan Dong in Shanxi University. His research is focused on the nanomaterials based biosensor and their biological applications.

Yating Meng received her master degree from Shanxi University in 2020 and now is pursuing her PhD degree in Environmental Science at Shanxi University. Her research interests mainly focus on the applications of fluorescent probe for biosenors.

Yuan Zhang is a postdoc of Institute of Environment Science at Shanxi Univ. He received his Ph.D. degree in Shanxi University in 2021. His research focus on the nanomaterials based biosensor and therapeutic device.

Qi Wang received his master degree from Sichuan University in 2014 and Ph.D. degree from Shanxi University in 2020. His research interests mainly focus on the nanomaterials-based application of fluorescent sensors in analytical chemistry.

Wenjing Lu is a lecturer of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in Shanxi University in 2019. Her research focuses on the construction and analytical applications of carbon dots based nanocomposites.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.
